Vivid cobalt-blue frog whose skin toxins warn predators away in Amazonian forest clearings.
The most toxic vertebrate known, its skin secretions once used by Emberá hunters to poison blowdarts.
Brilliantly multicolored New World vulture whose powerful beak opens carcasses for smaller scavengers.
Massive-billed hornbill topped with a hollow casque, an important seed-disperser in Asian forests.
Borneo's iconic hornbill, named for the upturned horn-like casque atop its bill.
Prehistoric-looking, foul-smelling bird that ferments leaves in an enlarged crop like a cow.
Near-flightless, ghost-grey forest bird found only on the island of New Caledonia.
Iridescent, peacock-hued turkey restricted to the forests of the Yucatán Peninsula.
Small Caribbean sea snail whose mantle displays a leopard-spotted pattern while it grazes on soft corals.
Odd, flattened Galápagos fish that "walks" along the seafloor on modified fins with bright red lips.
Deep-sea shark with a blade-like snout and jaws that snap forward to seize prey.
Eel-like, primitive shark from deep ocean waters with frilled, comb-like gill slits.
Tiny pelagic nudibranch that floats upside-down, storing stinging cells stolen from Portuguese man o' war.
Deep-sea cephalopod that cloaks itself in a webbed, cape-like mantle instead of ink when threatened.
Deepest-living octopus known, flapping ear-like fins to drift gracefully above the abyssal floor.
The heaviest invertebrate known, armed with swiveling hooks on its tentacles in the Southern Ocean depths.
Largest-known arthropod by leg span, scuttling across cold, deep waters off the Japanese coast.
World's largest land-living arthropod, powerful enough to crack open coconuts with its claws.
Vividly colored reef predator that strikes prey with some of the fastest limb movements in nature.
Blind crab covered in silky bristle-like setae, found clustered around deep hydrothermal vents.
Small striped marsupial that feeds almost exclusively on termites using a long sticky tongue.
Rabbit-eared, burrowing marsupial that has become a conservation icon of the Australian outback.
Australia's largest surviving carnivorous marsupial, hunting alone through forest at night.
Fearless, thick-skinned mustelid renowned for raiding beehives and standing up to far larger predators.
Long-legged wild cat that leaps high above grass to pounce on rodents and birds.
Agile, tree-dwelling wild cat able to rotate its ankles to climb down trunks headfirst.
Medium-sized spotted cat that hunts alone through dense understory across the Americas.
Slender, otter-like wild cat with a uniform coat, active by day unlike most small cats.
Pale, spiral-horned desert antelope superbly adapted to the driest reaches of the Sahara.
World's most endangered antelope, restricted to a sliver of grassland on the Kenya-Somalia border.
Sulawesi's strange pig whose upper tusks curve backward, sometimes piercing its own skull.
Bristle-maned wild pig now confined to fragments of forest on two Philippine islands.
Smallest and only high-altitude tapir species, browsing cloud forest and páramo of the northern Andes.
Long-legged, rabbit-like rodent that bounds across open Patagonian steppe in monogamous pairs.
Large forest rodent whose caching of seeds makes it a key disperser of tropical trees.
Long-snouted, ring-tailed relative of the raccoon that forages in noisy bands through the day.
Golden, prehensile-tailed rainforest mammal that feeds on nectar and fruit high in the canopy at night.
Large, energetic weasel relative that forages both on the ground and high in the trees.
Slender, spotted nocturnal carnivore that moves through trees and scrub with cat-like agility.
Large, masked nocturnal civet whose musky scent gland secretions were historically prized in perfumery.
Small insectivorous fox whose oversized ears pinpoint termites and beetle larvae underground.
Stocky canid resembling a raccoon that is one of the few dog relatives to hibernate.
Slow-moving nocturnal primate whose bite delivers a rare venomous toxin among mammals.
Gliding mammal covered in a wide skin membrane, sailing over 100 meters between forest trees.
Large gliding rodent that spreads a furred membrane to soar silently between forest trees at night.
Long-tailed desert rodent that bounds on greatly elongated hind legs across sand and scrub.
Kangaroo-like rodent that hops on powerful hind legs across open African savanna at night.
Desert rodent with ears larger than its own head, hopping across the Gobi's gravel plains at night.
Smallest anteater species, a golden-furred, nocturnal specialist that lives entirely in the trees.
Medium-sized anteater with a prehensile tail, equally at home climbing trees or foraging on the ground.
One of the slowest mammals alive, hosting algae in its fur that provides camouflage in the canopy.
One of the rarest primates on Earth, a snow-white lemur confined to Madagascar's northeastern rainforest.
Strikingly dimorphic lemur in which males are jet black and females a rich chestnut brown.
One of the world's smallest primates, foraging alone through Madagascar's forest understory at night.
Madagascar's most familiar carnivore, a russet mongoose relative that forages along forest streams.
The world's largest tenrec, a spiny insectivore that raises the biggest litters of any mammal.
Brightly striped tenrec that communicates by vibrating specialized quills on its back.
Nearly blind, velvet-furred burrower that "swims" through loose soil hunting earthworms.
Wetland burrower whose fleshy nasal tentacles form the fastest-touch sensory organ known.
Semi-aquatic insectivore with a flexible trunk-like snout, restricted to fast mountain streams.
Small, tailless relative of rabbits whose burrows are a keystone habitat across the Tibetan Plateau.
The 'Asian unicorn', a forest bovid so rare it was only discovered by science in 1992.
Largest land mammal native to Central America, wading through rivers and dense lowland forest.
Slow-moving, prehensile-tailed marsupial with a blotchy coat that camouflages it in New Guinea's canopy.
Large, shaggy cuscus endemic to Sulawesi that moves with slow, deliberate climbs through the canopy.
Bushy-tailed marsupial with a golden coat variant, common in forests across Australia.
Small rat-kangaroo that spreads fungal spores through the soil, a keystone ecosystem engineer.
Rust-colored tree-dwelling kangaroo that climbs the cloud forests of the Huon Peninsula.
Large, spine-covered egg-laying mammal that probes New Guinea leaf litter with an elongated snout.
Small, clouded-leopard-patterned wild cat with an exceptionally long tail used for canopy balance.
Semi-aquatic wild cat with partially webbed paws that hunts fish in Southeast Asian wetlands.
Southeast Asia's most heavily trafficked mammal, curling into an armored ball of overlapping scales.
Africa's smallest wild cat and a formidably successful hunter relative to its tiny size.
World's smallest deer species, browsing dense temperate rainforest understory in Chile and Argentina.
Large, colonial burrowing rodent related to chinchillas that forms extensive warrens on the pampas.
Bald, scarlet-faced Amazonian monkey whose flushed skin signals health to potential mates.
Short-tailed Amazonian monkey with a strikingly bare, crimson face amid flooded forest habitat.
Silver-grey gibbon confined to fragmented rainforest remnants on the island of Java.
India and Myanmar's only ape, swinging through canopy with long, powerful arms and a loud duet call.
Gliding mammal of Malaysian forests capable of covering long distances between trees on a single leap.
Vividly multicolored squirrel far larger than most, making spectacular leaps across forest canopy gaps.
One of the world's rarest carnivores, possibly surviving only in a few fragments of the Western Ghats.
Loud-calling gibbon in which males are solid black and females turn golden-orange with age.
One of Madagascar's largest lemurs, giving birth to litters stashed in leaf nests high in the canopy.
Small forest antelope striped like a zebra, restricted to the Upper Guinea forests of West Africa.
Large, round-bodied Amazonian fish related to piranha but equipped with blunt, seed-crushing teeth.
One of the largest freshwater fish in the world, surfacing periodically to gulp air with a lung-like bladder.
'Living fossil' fish thought extinct for 65 million years until a specimen was caught off South Africa in 1938.
Angola's national symbol, a huge black antelope with dramatically curved scimitar horns, found only in a handful of central Angolan reserves.
Elusive West African sirenian that grazes on coastal seagrass and mangrove roots in brackish estuaries and lagoons.
